title: The Grafting Hour
author: C. M. Holloway
description: When an ancient witch's corpse is unearthed from the moor, children begin to vanish. Maeve, a village outcast, discovers the witch needs their body parts to stitch herself a new form. With only two children left, Maeve must face the horror beneath the peat before the final ritual is complete.

content.text: The men found her on a Tuesday, when the fog lay so thick on the moor it felt like drowning. They were cutting peat, their spades slicing through the black, fibrous earth with the wet, sucking sound that had been the heartbeat of Briarwick for centuries. Old Ferris struck something hard, harder than the usual roots, and when he bent to haul it free he thought at first it was a fallen log. Then he saw the hand. It was curled, black, the fingers still bent in a clutching shape, the nails long and strangely intact. He screamed, and the fog ate the sound before it could travel far.

They pulled her out with ropes and a reverent, terrified care. The body was a woman, or had been. The peat had preserved her so perfectly that her skin was like cured leather, stretched tight over high cheekbones and a jaw that still held a suggestion of sharp beauty. Her hair, long and reddish, was plaited with strands of dead heather. But it was the brand on her forehead that stilled every tongue: a circle bisected by a jagged line, burned into the flesh, the mark of the Cailleach witches who had been hunted into extinction four hundred years ago. No one spoke her name then, but it hung in the air like a noxious smoke. Morwenna.

Maeve was not there to see the unearthing. She was in the cottage at the edge of the village, grinding herbs for her grandmother, who was too old to stand and whose mind wandered through memories like a moth through a dark room. Maeve was fourteen, an orphan twice over — her mother dead in childbirth, her father lost to the sea — and she had learned young that the village viewed her with a sidelong suspicion. She had her grandmother’s red hair, and her grandmother had always been called a wise woman, which was one whisper away from a worse word. When the news of the bog-body reached her, carried by a white-faced boy who had watched the men carry the corpse to the old tithe barn, Maeve’s grandmother sat up in her bed and spoke for the first time in a week. ‘The Grafting Hour,’ she said, her voice like dry reeds. ‘She’ll be looking for parts.’

content.caption: The moor where the body of Morwenna was found, still breathing the ancient dark.

content.text: Maeve’s little brother, Finn, was only seven. He had their mother’s eyes and their father’s restless curiosity, and he did not understand why Maeve refused to let him out of her sight after the body was found. ‘It’s just a dead lady,’ he said, building a tower of river stones on the cottage floor. ‘Dead ladies can’t hurt you.’ Maeve, remembering the brand and the way the men’s faces had looked when they described the hand still clutching, said nothing. But that night, she tied a red thread around Finn’s wrist, the way her grandmother had taught her, and whispered the old words of warding that tasted like iron on her tongue.

Tom Eckersley was the first. He was nine, a sturdy, loud-laughing boy who helped his father tend the sheep on the high pasture. He went missing on a Thursday evening, sent out to close the gate, and when he did not return by supper his mother raised the alarm. The village searched all night with lanterns and dogs, calling his name into a wind that seemed to swallow the syllables. They found him at dawn, laid out in a shallow dip in the moor like a gift, his body undirtied but his chest torn open with a precision that made the men who found him sick. His heart was gone.

The village constable, a blustering man named Harker, called it a wild beast. A wolf, perhaps, though no wolf had been seen in the shire for a hundred years. Maeve stood at the edge of the grieving crowd and saw the clean cuts, the way the ribcage had been opened as if by a surgeon. She saw, too, the faint smear of grey dust on Tom’s eyelids — the same grey dust that had clung to the bog-body’s hair. That night, her grandmother spoke again, her eyes sharp and lucid. ‘The heart is the first anchor. She needs seven parts. Heart, hands, eyes, tongue, liver, feet, and the seventh... the seventh is the voice. Seven children, or she cannot walk. You must find the other marks before she does.’

Maeve began to watch. She had always been a watcher, the invisible girl at the edge of things, and now she turned that skill into a desperate vigilance. She noted the children who had been born under certain moons, the ones who carried any trace of the old blood. The witch would not take just anyone. She needed innocence, and she needed a thread of the same old magic that had been woven into the moor itself. There were ten such children in the village. Ten had become nine with Tom’s death. Maeve’s brother Finn was one of them. She marked their names on a scrap of paper and hid it inside her sleeve, close to her own racing pulse.

chapters.title: Chapter 3 — The Hands and the Eyes

content.text: The second murder happened on a Sunday, while the village was at church. The girl was called Anwen, a shy, quiet thing of ten who had beautiful hands, her mother always said, the hands of a pianist. She had stayed home with a fever, and when her family returned, they found her on the kitchen floor, her slender wrists ending in raw, bloodless stumps. Her hands had been taken. The grey dust was in her hair. The village’s fear turned to a seething, directionless fury. Some wanted to burn the tithe barn where the bog-body lay under a shroud; others whispered that the body had already been seen moving, that the shroud was empty, that something walked the moor in the shape of a woman stitched from shadow and peat.

Maeve went to the barn herself, slipping away while her grandmother slept. The body was still there, laid out on a trestle table, but it had changed. The left hand, which had been black and withered, was now pale and plump, a child’s hand grafted crudely to the ancient wrist with seams of what looked like dried sinew. The eyes, which had been sunken hollows, now held a faint, milky glimmer. The witch was rebuilding herself, piece by stolen piece. Maeve stood in the cold barn, her breath pluming, and felt a presence settle against her mind like a cold hand. A voice, not sound but a pressure of meaning, slid into her thoughts: *I will have the last two before the moon is new. You cannot stop the Grafting. She who was burned will rise.* Maeve fled into the fog, her heart hammering.

The next to die was a boy called Daveth, a cheerful twelve-year-old with a squint. They found him near the old stone circle on the moor, his eyes neatly removed, the sockets filled with the same grey dust. The villagers began to lock their doors and refuse to let their children out of sight, but the witch’s methods grew more cunning. She could whisper through keyholes, mimic a mother’s voice, send dreams of lost toys and friendly dogs that led children to the edge of the bog. Maeve, armed with her grandmother’s old book of protections, went from house to house, marking doorframes with a paste of rowan ash and salt, but the witch was patient and the village was large, and Maeve was just one girl.

chapters.title: Chapter 4 — The Tongue and the Liver

content.text: The fourth was a little girl named Bronwen, who had a lisp and a habit of chattering like a sparrow. They found her tongue gone. The fifth was a boy called Ewan, the butcher’s son, whose liver was taken while he slept in a locked room, his parents in the bed across the hall, hearing nothing. By then, the village had fractured into factions: some wanted to dig up the bog entirely and burn whatever else they found, some accused Maeve’s grandmother of having called the witch back, and some simply fled, packing their carts and vanishing into the mist, though Maeve suspected the moor would not let them go. The road out of Briarwick had a way of curving back on itself.

Maeve sat in the cottage with her grandmother’s book open on her knees, the pages brittle as autumn leaves. The book spoke of Morwenna in fractured, terrified script: a witch of the Cailleach order who had once tried to become immortal by harvesting the youth of others. She had been burned at the stake in 1623, but the flames had not taken her body. Her followers had swapped the corpse, burying it in the peat to preserve it until the stars aligned for her return. The ritual she needed was the Seaming — seven parts from seven children of the blood, stitched into her form with thread spun from grave-hair, under a new moon. Once complete, she would walk again, not as a woman but as a vessel of hunger, and the village would become a larder. Maeve counted the days until the new moon: five. Five days, and there were still two children on her list who remained untouched. Her brother Finn, and a baby girl called Alys, not yet two years old, whose grandmother had once been a midwife and knew the old ways.

content.caption: The edge of the moor, where the witch's whispers seemed loudest after dark.

content.text: The sixth was taken the next night: a girl called Liddy, whose feet were described by her mother as the swiftest in the valley. Her body was found at the foot of the church steps, her feet removed with the same terrible precision, the grey dust thick as a warning. Maeve knew then that she was running out of time. The witch had five parts — heart, hands, eyes, tongue, liver — and now she needed feet and a voice. Finn’s voice was still a boy’s clear treble, and Alys’s babble was the last unsullied sound of a newborn soul. Maeve made a choice. She would not wait for the witch to come to them. She would go into the moor, into the heart of the old magic, and find the place where the witch’s true self hid while her body mended.

She left Finn with their grandmother, drawing a triple circle of salt around the cottage and repeating the strongest warding words she knew. Then she took her father’s old silver knife, a lantern filled with oil, and the book, and walked into the mist. The moor was a breathing, watchful thing. The ground squelched under her boots, and the fog twisted into shapes that seemed to reach for her. She followed the lines of the old ley paths her grandmother had once described, invisible tracks that hummed with a low, ancient frequency. They led her to a place where the peat gave way to a deep, black pool, its surface mirror-still, reflecting no light.

The witch rose from the water like a nightmare assembling itself. She was no longer the leather-skinned corpse from the barn. She had become a grotesque patchwork, a woman-shaped thing of mis-matched skin tones and fresh scars. Anwen’s small hands dangled from her wrists, flexing and grasping. Daveth’s eyes, milky and unfocused, rolled in their sockets. She opened her mouth — Bronwen’s tongue, pink and alive, moved inside it — and spoke with a voice that was not her own. ‘You have brought me nothing, little bird. But I will have your brother’s voice. And the infant’s feet. Then I will walk.’

Maeve did not answer with words. She began to chant, reading from the book a counter-ritual that her grandmother had marked with a faded sprig of heather. The words were old, older than the Cailleach, older than the peat, a binding that could stitch a soul back into its original clay. The witch screamed — a sound that was part Ewan’s liver-struck groan and part Tom’s heart-stopped gasp — and lunged. But Maeve held her ground, the silver knife raised, and as the witch’s patchwork hands reached for her throat, Maeve drew the blade across her own palm and slammed her bleeding hand against the brand on the witch’s forehead. The blood of a living child of the bloodline, freely given, was the one ingredient Morwenna could not steal. The binding took hold.

The witch convulsed. The stolen parts began to wither, the grafts unravelling like rotten thread. Anwen’s hands fell to the ground and crumbled; Daveth’s eyes melted; Bronwen’s tongue blackened and fell from the witch’s mouth. Morwenna shrieked, a long, spiralling cry that echoed across the moor, and then her body collapsed back into the form it had worn in the bog — black, withered, inert. The pool swallowed her, and the mist began to thin. Maeve fell to her knees, her hand still bleeding, and watched as the first pale light of dawn touched the water. The witch was bound again, her resurrection halted. But the book was clear: the binding would hold only as long as the bloodline held. Maeve would carry the mark of the brand on her own palm now, a silver scar, and one day, when she died, the witch might rise again. But that was a problem for another lifetime.

She staggered back to the village to find Finn and Alys safe, the circles unbroken. The remaining children, those whose parts had not been taken, wept with relief. The village looked at Maeve with new eyes — some with gratitude, some with a deeper, more frightened awe. The tithe barn was empty; the body had dissolved into dust. The Grafting Hour had passed, the new moon had risen, and the children of Briarwick had survived. But on quiet nights, Maeve could still hear a whisper from the moor, a faint, patient promise: *I will wait. The body remembers.*

content.caption: The dawn after the binding, the moor silent but never truly still.
